title: Local-to-global heating crossover in chains of nanomagnets: A two-scale analytical framework
abstract: We develop a two-scale analytical formalism to study heat generation and thermal transport in one-dimensional systems of nanomagnets subjected to a uniform alternating magnetic field. At the nanoscale, each nanomagnet acts as a localized, temperature-dependent heat source governed by its magnetic response, dipolar interactions, and interfacial coupling to the matrix, characterized by a nanoscale volumetric loss coefficient $L_m$. After spatial and temporal averaging, we obtain a coarse-grained assembly-scale equation with effective heating terms and a macroscopic loss coefficient $L_N$.   Using modal decomposition, we solve both equations exactly under Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions and establish explicit conditions for a local-to-global heating crossover; this is governed by the competition between heat generation, diffusion, dipolar coupling, and hierarchical losses. The crossover is quantified through the spatial correlation length and temperature variance, with stability criteria incorporating both diffusion and nanoscale losses. The coarse-graining procedure is derived rigorously, and its systematic approximation errors are quantified.   For prototypical magnetic hyperthermia systems, such as magnetite nanomagnets in water, our formalism reveals that realistic parameters place these systems firmly in the collective heating regime, with local temperature variations at the $\simμ$K level, which is currently unresolvable experimentally. The continuum Fourier description used here is validated by a Knudsen-number analysis ($\mathrm{Kn} \ll 1$ for amorphous polymer and aqueous matrices).
summary: This work establishes a comprehensive two-scale analytical framework to model heat transfer in nanomagnet chains under alternating magnetic fields. By rigorously coarse-graining the nanoscale physics, the authors predict that realistic systems exhibit collective heating, with temperature fluctuations at the microkelvin level. The methodology is valuable for understanding energy dissipation in complex, coupled mesoscopic systems.
main_problem: The paper develops a two-scale framework to study heat generation and thermal transport in chains of nanomagnets subjected to an alternating magnetic field, focusing on the crossover between localized and global heating regimes.
main_result: For realistic magnetic hyperthermia systems, the model predicts the system operates in a collective heating regime, with local temperature variations at the $\sim\mu	ext{K}$ level, which is currently experimentally unresolvable.
method: The authors employ a rigorous two-scale analytical formalism involving spatial and temporal coarse-graining, solving the resulting coupled equations using modal decomposition.
model_or_system: The system consists of one-dimensional chains of nanomagnets (e.g., magnetite) embedded in a matrix, where heat generation is modeled as a localized, temperature-dependent source term governed by magnetic losses.
key_observables: Spatial correlation length, temperature variance, and the crossover condition governed by the competition between heat generation, diffusion, and dipolar coupling.
important_parameters: Nanoscale volumetric loss coefficient ($L_m$), macroscopic loss coefficient ($L_N$), Knudsen number ($Kn$), and the dimensionless coupling strength ($\gamma$).
main_assumptions: The continuum Fourier description is valid because the Knudsen number is much less than one ($Kn \ll 1$) for the matrices considered.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of Specific Loss Power (SLP) on frequency and volume, and compare the thermal behavior under Dirichlet (fixed boundary) versus Neumann (insulated boundary) conditions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from deriving the nanoscale heat equation, through the rigorous process of spatial and temporal coarse-graining to obtain an assembly-scale equation, and finally analyzing the stability and crossover criteria using modal decomposition.
why_interesting: The rigorous development of a multi-scale, non-equilibrium thermal transport model, especially one that quantifies the crossover between local and global energy dissipation, provides advanced tools applicable to open quantum systems where environmental coupling and spatial averaging are crucial.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Local temperature profiles θ(ξ, τ) around three source sites (ξ = 1, 28, 54) at instants τ = 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 for the illustrative parameter set (Table III). Vertical dashed lines mark the interparticle spacing ∆ξ = 1. (Upper panel) Dirichlet BCs. (Lower panel) Neumann BCs.

FIG. 2: Detrended local profiles δθ(ξ, τ) around three source sites (ξ = 1, 28, 54) for the physical magnetite–PMMA parameter set (Table I) at τ = 10, 50, 100, 200. (Upper panel) Dirichlet BCs. (Lower panel) Neumann BCs.

FIG. 3: Local profiles at the central source vs. interparticle separation d. Detrended profile δθ(ξ −ξsrc, τ) for the physical magnetite–PMMA parameter set (same as Fig. 2) at τ = 100, for d = 36, 50, 80 nm. Note that β ∝d2 and Λ = L/d vary with d; the annotation box shows the reference values at d = 36 nm. (Left panel) Dirichlet BCs. (Right panel) Neumann BCs.
FIG. 4: Temperature history θ(τ) at five source positions from near-edge (ξ0 = 1) to chain centre (ξ0 = 10) (Λ = 20, N = 21, ˜a = 0.4, ˜b = −0.5, β = 0.015).

Figure 6 shows θ(ξ, τ) at τ = 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 for Λ = 42, N = 7, ∆ξ = 7, ˜b = −0.52, β = 0.017 (localized regime, well below ˜bc). Both panels share the same set of source positions; the faint vertical dashed lines mark each source site for orientation. Under DBC [Fig. 6, left], the profiles grow uniformly across the interior of the chain, with the edge sources visibly suppressed relative to the bulk. We see that the envelope of the peaks is nearly flat in the interior at each time, thus confirming that the fundamental Dirich- let mode sin(πξ/Λ) spreads the heating broadly.
